content.assessment: Reforming admissions processes without altering track structure leaves the fundamental stratification mechanism intact—the tracks themselves, operating at ages 10-13, remain the structural feature independently linked to inequality. Admissions-process reform could plausibly reduce some socioeconomic bias operating through private preparation access, but the evidence on self-exclusion suggests disadvantaged families' non-participation is driven by strategic/behavioral decisions rather than exam design alone, implying limited reach for this lever. The underlying segregation channel—school-type stratification correlated with family SES—would likely persist since the track structure and its selective character are preserved.
opportunities: Could incrementally reduce one component of SES-based access inequality (preparation-access advantage) without requiring full structural negotiation
strengths: Targets a specific access barrier (private exam preparation) that plausibly compounds SES-based sorting independent of ability
Lower structural disruption than full track abolition, potentially preserving continuity of access for currently enrolled students
threats: Self-exclusion behavior rooted in family strategy rather than exam design suggests reform may have limited effect on actual segregation outcomes, per the cited mechanism evidence
Segregation between Hungarian schools has strengthened substantially over recent decades; a non-structural intervention operating only at the margin of admissions is unlikely to reverse this trend
weaknesses: Does not address the structural existence of early tracking itself, which is independently linked to inequality beyond admissions-process features
Track-based segregation and stratification by SES, documented as a major channel for Hungary's exceptionally strong achievement gradient, would likely persist unchanged
